categoryDescription: Looks at the ETF's past performance: multi-year returns, recent momentum, calendar-year consistency, AUM size & operational scale, and standing inside its category peer group. Five factors apply to every ETF group. The `factorDescription` says what is measured and gives the generic Pass/Fail rule. `groupInstructions` gives the group-specific perspective — how to frame the benchmark, when technicals matter, and which structural checks (distribution stability, NAV erosion, roll cost, daily-leverage decay) apply. When the two seem to conflict, follow `groupInstructions`.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at multi-year compound growth (5 / 10 / 15 / 20Y CAGR and trailing returns) and asks whether the fund beat its benchmark over each long window. For funds younger than 5 years, use only the periods you have and note the short history. Pass when CAGR matches or beats the benchmark across most windows, or stays within tracking tolerance for a passive index fund. Fail when CAGR trails the benchmark across several long windows without a clear mandate-based reason.
factors.factorMetrics: cagr5y, cagr10y, cagr15y, cagr20y, return5y, return10y, returnsAnnual, returnsTrailing, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare CAGR to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If no index is given, pick the most suitable benchmark for this ETF. ALWAYS also compare to the S&P 500 — even when the fund tracks a different index, retail investors use the S&P 500 as their default reference. For style-tilted funds (value, growth, dividend, low-vol, quality), the tilt should beat the parent broad-market index over long windows when its style is in favor. Plain Large Blend / Total Market funds should sit within tracking tolerance of the benchmark.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare CAGR to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If no index is given, pick the most suitable sector or theme benchmark. ALWAYS also compare to the S&P 500 — this is the retail mandate test. A sector or theme that just tracked the broad market over 10Y has not delivered on its thesis.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Long-horizon CAGR is the daily-reset decay test. Quote the underlying's CAGR × stated leverage as the textbook expectation, then show the actual result and call the gap compounding decay. State plainly: these are short-term trading vehicles, never buy-and-hold. The 'how much would $10k be today' framing does not apply here. Do not require a direct comparison to the S&P 500.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare CAGR to the spot benchmark given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick the most suitable spot reference for this commodity or digital asset. For futures-based commodity wrappers, the gap between the spot price and the fund is NAV erosion from contango / roll cost — call this out plainly. For physical-backed or spot-based funds, note the small tracking gap vs spot from custody and management costs.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Long-term CAGR is the mandate test. Covered-call funds should deliver yield + capped upside + a cushion in down markets — verify all three. Long-short / market-neutral should show equity-like-or-lower CAGR with materially lower drawdown. Managed-futures should show crisis alpha in stress years. Compare TOTAL return (with distributions reinvested) to the underlying equity benchmark given in the prompt (or a suitable benchmark if none is given) and to a high-dividend equity reference. Flag a flat or negative 10Y price-only return paired with a positive total return — that means distributions are partly return-of-capital.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare CAGR to a same-period 60/40 mix (broad US equity + US aggregate bond) and to the allocation peer-category median. Also compare to the DIY equivalent a retail reader could build (an aggressive mix is ~80% broad equity + 20% core bond; a conservative mix is ~30% equity + 70% bond) — the fund's value-add is automatic rebalancing, not alpha. Mandate-band check: moderate ~5-7%, conservative ~4-5%, aggressive ~7-9%. Target-date funds should track the issuer's published glide path. For tactical / global allocation, ask whether active calls beat a passive 60/40 over multi-year windows.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare CAGR to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick a suitable duration-matched benchmark for this ETF (core aggregate bond, a Treasury duration band, national / state muni, or TIPS). For muni funds, also quote tax-equivalent CAGR at ~32% federal (plus state exemption for in-state holders). For TIPS, separate real vs nominal returns. If the fund's yield is below cash / HYSA, say plainly the reason to hold it is optionality (price rally if rates fall), not yield.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare CAGR to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick a suitable credit benchmark for this sub-asset (high-yield corporate, bank loan, preferred, EM debt, or convertible). Also quote a same-period 60/40 CAGR — retail's honest question is whether they were paid for taking real default and subordination risk. Translate 'high yield' plainly: below-investment-grade credit with real default risk. For high-yield muni funds in this group, also quote tax-equivalent CAGR at ~32% federal (plus state exemption for in-state holders).

factors.factorDescription: Looks at recent returns over 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y. Separate short-term momentum (1M, 3M) from trailing performance (6M, 1Y) — a strong 1Y with weak recent months is usually a normal pullback, not broad weakness. Where entry timing matters for the fund's typical holder, also read the technical signals (MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200, RSI daily / weekly / monthly, distance from 52w high / low and ATH / ATL); where it does not, keep technical commentary brief. Every short-term number needs the same-period number for the benchmark. Pass when short-term performance matches or beats the benchmark and the trend is decision-useful for the fund's typical holding horizon. Fail when materially lagging across multiple recent windows without a mandate-based reason.
factors.factorMetrics: return1m, return3m, return6m, returnYtd, return1y, returnsAnnual, ma20, ma50, ma150, ma200, ma50ChgPercent, ma200ChgPercent, rsi, rsiW, rsiM, high52wChg, low52wChg, athChgPercent, atlChgPercent, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick the most suitable broad-equity benchmark for this ETF. ALWAYS also compare to the S&P 500 — retail uses it as the default reference even when the fund tracks a different index. Flag whether near-term weakness is fund-specific (lagging the benchmark) or a broad-market move that hit every peer. MA / RSI signals are usually noise for buy-and-hold broad-equity — keep technical commentary brief unless price is at a clear extreme (monthly RSI >70 or <30).
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare each window to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick a suitable sector / theme benchmark. ALWAYS also compare to the S&P 500 — retail wants to see whether the sector bet is currently beating or fading vs the broad market. Sector cycles drive forward returns: read MA50 / MA200 position and RSI to gauge entry timing. Flag overbought (monthly RSI >70) or oversold (<30) and tie it to where the sector sits in its macro cycle.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Short-term return IS the decision frame here. Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the stated daily-leverage multiple of the unleveraged underlying — a 3x fund should be roughly 3x the underlying's same-period move minus reset slippage. Flag any large gap as path-dependency loss. The honest comparison is 'vs not holding this at all' — most retail investors should not hold these beyond a few trading days. Read price vs MA20 / 50 / 150 / 200 and RSI (monthly RSI >75 is stretched even by trading standards). Frame current entry against the 52-week range.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare to the spot underlying given in the prompt (`indexName`) over each window. If none is given, pick a suitable spot reference for this commodity or digital asset. For futures-based commodity wrappers, show the spot vs fund gap and call out roll cost (contango / backwardation). These are momentum-driven asset classes — quote MA50 / MA200 position and RSI (monthly RSI >70 stretched, <30 washout) and frame current price against the ATH and the 52-week range.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y TOTAL return to the underlying equity benchmark given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick a suitable equity benchmark for the strategy's option-writing universe. Distribution composition matters — option-premium income, ordinary dividends, short-term capital gains, return of capital. Headline yield alone misleads; flag whether ROC is propping the yield while NAV erodes. MA / RSI is noise here — keep technical commentary minimal.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to a same-period 60/40 mix (broad US equity + US aggregate bond) and to the allocation peer-category median. For target-date funds, also compare to the issuer's published peer group for the dated year. MA / RSI signals are noise for allocation funds — keep technical commentary minimal.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick a suitable duration-matched benchmark (core aggregate bond, a Treasury duration band, national / state muni, or TIPS). Flag whether near-term moves are rate-driven (parallel with peers) or fund-specific (active calls, tracking drift). Distribution should track SEC yield — a large gap signals smoothing. MA / RSI is noise here.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare 1M / 3M / 6M / YTD / 1Y to the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`). If none is given, pick a suitable credit benchmark (high-yield corporate, bank loan, preferred, EM debt, or convertible). Flag whether short-term weakness is spread-widening that hit the whole sub-asset class or fund-specific. Distribution moves with reference rates and default cycles.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at how stable returns have been across periods and calendar years. Quote calendar-year hit rate (how often the fund had a positive year), worst single year, and percentile-rank trend year-over-year (cite the actual movement, e.g. `14 → 87 → 18`, not just 'volatile'). Benchmark-matched bad year rule: a passive fund whose worst year is in line with its benchmark and peer category is not a Fail — that is the asset class moving, not fund failure. For income-paying funds, also check distribution stability: did the headline yield hold up, ratchet down, or get propped up by return-of-capital? A flat total return on top of a steadily eroding NAV is not real consistency. Pass when the calendar-year pattern fits the group's typical dispersion and distributions (where relevant) held up. Fail when the fund swings materially harder than its benchmark, OR distributions were cut sharply, OR total return is being propped up by ROC.
factors.factorMetrics: returnsAnnual, return1y, return3y, return5y, return10y, percentileRanks, dividendTtm, divGrowth3y, divGrowth5y, divYears, divGrYears, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, change1y, change5y, change10y, indexName

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Show calendar-year hit rate, worst single year, and percentile-rank trend against the benchmark index given in the prompt (`indexName`); if none is given, pick a suitable broad-equity benchmark. ALWAYS also frame the calendar-year pattern against the S&P 500 with actual numbers, not vague prose — retail uses it as the default reference for any equity fund. Quote the percentile-rank trajectory year-by-year as a sequence (e.g. `6 → 51 → 32`) — a deteriorating run matters even when the latest year looks fine. For broad-equity dividend / income tilts, also check whether the distribution held up year-over-year.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Sector and country returns swing harder than the broad market. Quote the worst single year and whether it lined up with a broad-market bad year or was sector-specific. ALWAYS also reference the S&P 500 calendar-year pattern over the same window with actual numbers so retail sees the trade-off vs holding the broad market. Quote the percentile-rank trajectory year-by-year as a sequence (e.g. `6 → 51 → 32`) — a deteriorating run matters even when the latest year looks fine.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Consistency is structurally poor here. Quote calendar-year wins vs losses, the worst single year, and recovery duration. Retail needs to see plainly that consistency is not a design feature of these products — reinforce the short-term-only warning every iteration.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Calendar-year dispersion is wide for this asset class — quote hit rate, worst year, and the big up years. Also reference the S&P 500 calendar-year pattern over the same window so retail sees the trade-off vs holding equities. For futures-based wrappers, a steadily declining 5Y / 10Y price-only return alongside a positive distribution-yielded total return is roll-cost NAV erosion in disguise.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Show how yield + capped upside translated into total return each calendar year, including down years where option premium did NOT offset the underlying loss. Quote per-share distribution year-by-year, the latest-year ROC share, and the divergence between total return and price-only return — a flat-to-positive total return on top of a steadily declining NAV is structural NAV erosion. Compare the worst calendar year to both the underlying equity benchmark AND a high-dividend equity reference.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Smooth-ride delivery is the core mandate. Quote calendar-year hit rate, the worst single year, and a same-period 100% broad-equity worst year side-by-side — a moderate-allocation fund should have a materially smaller worst year than pure equity. For target-date glide paths, compare to the issuer's own glide-path target. The bond-sleeve distribution should track its yield; flag any abrupt drops.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Quote calendar-year hit rate and the worst calendar year. Compare worst year to a duration-matched Treasury or core-bond reference — long-duration funds will have lost meaningfully in rate-shock years, intermediate less, and ultrashort / MMF barely at all. Distribution should closely track SEC yield; large gaps signal smoothing or de-accumulated coupons.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Quote calendar-year hit rate and worst single year. Show per-share distribution year-by-year, especially around credit-stress windows. Break out the ROC share for preferreds and convertibles where it is structurally larger. Compare the worst year to the matching credit benchmark given in the prompt (or a suitable one if none is given).

factors.factorDescription: Looks at the fund's total assets under management as a market-validated read on past performance and operational durability. AUM is the dollar-weighted vote investors have cast on this fund over its history — a fund that has reached and held meaningful scale has earned that scale through past returns, distribution stability, and continued investor confidence. Three things matter: (1) absolute AUM size — funds above ~$1B carry strong validation and operational depth; $250M-$1B is healthy and viable; $50-250M is functional but not validated at scale; below ~$50M is small enough that operational economics get thin. (2) Scale vs category peers — a $500M fund is large in a niche thematic category but small in broad-equity; always frame size against the fund's specific category. (3) Trading friction that AUM supports — daily dollar volume above ~$1M and bid-ask spreads in line with category norm are the practical tests that scale is translating into retail-usable liquidity. AUM size is past-performance evidence, not forward survivability — a small fund that has held steady AUM is showing acceptance, while closure-threshold framing properly belongs in future outlook. Pass when AUM is comfortably above the category's scale threshold AND trading friction is acceptable for retail. Fail when AUM sits well below category-typical scale AND trading friction would materially tax retail round-trips.
factors.factorMetrics: aum, totalAssets, sharesOut, volume, avgVolume, dollarVol, marketBidAskSpread, inceptionDate, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Largest scale of any group — major US large-cap passive funds run hundreds of billions in AUM (VOO, VTI, IVV, SPY all above $500B). Broad-equity at this scale is essentially zero operational concern. For factor-tilt, dividend, or international broad-equity funds, $5B+ is established and well-scaled; $1-5B is healthy; $250M-$1B is functional. Below $250M for a newer broad-equity fund is small relative to category norm — note context. Daily dollar volume on major funds runs into the billions; thinner trading on smaller broad-equity funds is the more practical retail concern than absolute AUM.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Wide AUM dispersion across this group. Major sector ETFs (XLK, XLF, XLV, VGT) run $20-100B+. Mid-tier sector and large thematic ETFs sit at $1-10B. Niche thematic ETFs commonly sit at $50-500M. Above ~$500M for a thematic ETF is meaningful validation that the theme has earned investor money. Below ~$50M for a thematic that has been live for 3+ years signals retail hasn't found the thesis compelling. Daily volume and spread are the practical liquidity tests — many thematic ETFs have decent AUM but thin daily volume.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Concentrated AUM — the major leveraged products (TQQQ, SOXL, UPRO, SQQQ) run $5-25B and have enormous daily volume. Smaller leveraged products on single stocks or narrow indices commonly sit at $50-500M. Above $500M signals durable trader interest; below $50M signals niche-product status with thinner daily volume. Daily dollar volume matters more than AUM for these products because the use case is rapid trading — a small fund with poor liquidity is unusable even if directionally correct.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Major precious-metal ETFs (GLD, IAU, SLV) and spot Bitcoin ETFs (IBIT, FBTC) run $20-100B+. Mid-tier metal and futures-based commodity ETFs sit at $1-10B. Smaller single-commodity and second-tier crypto wrappers commonly sit at $100M-$1B. Above $1B for any wrapper is well-scaled and operationally durable; $250M-$1B is healthy for newer launches. Below $100M with a meaningful operating history signals weak adoption. Custody and audit cost structure for spot crypto and physical-backed wrappers benefits from scale, so AUM matters slightly more here than for plain equity.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Wide dispersion. Category leaders (JEPI, JEPQ, QYLD, SPYI, QQQI) run $5-40B and have earned their scale through strong retail adoption. Mid-tier covered-call and defined-outcome ETFs sit at $500M-$5B. The 2023-2025 launch wave produced many sub-$500M derivative-income funds. Above $1B is strong validation; $250M-$1B is functional; below $250M for a fund 2+ years old signals retail hasn't preferred this option-mechanic vs the category leaders.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Target-date funds get most AUM from 401(k) plan defaults — major series (Vanguard, BlackRock, Fidelity, T. Rowe Price) hold tens of billions per vintage. Allocation ETFs (AOA, AOM, AOR, AOK) run $1-5B per mix. Tactical-allocation ETFs sit at $100M-$2B. Above $1B is well-scaled; $250M-$1B functional; below $250M for an allocation ETF 2+ years old is small relative to peer norm. AUM is mostly mechanical here (sticky 401(k) flows), so absolute size matters more than recent changes.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Major core bond ETFs (AGG, BND) run $90-110B+. Treasury ETFs (TLT, IEF, VGIT) run $20-50B. National muni ETFs (MUB, VTEB) run $30-40B. Single-state muni and specialty duration ETFs commonly sit at $100M-$2B. Above $1B for any IG bond ETF is well-scaled; $250M-$1B is healthy; below $100M for a 3+ year-old IG fund is small. Spread and daily volume are tighter for major funds — practical retail liquidity is meaningfully better at scale.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Major HY and preferred ETFs (HYG, JNK, USHY, PFF) run $10-25B. EM debt (EMB), bank loan (BKLN), convertible (CWB) ETFs sit at $2-15B. Newer active-credit and specialty ETFs sit at $250M-$2B. Above $1B is well-scaled; $250M-$1B is functional; below $250M for a 3+ year-old credit ETF is small relative to category. Credit ETFs benefit meaningfully from scale because the underlying basket is less liquid — bid-ask spreads narrow with AUM.

factors.factorDescription: Looks at where the fund sits inside its category peer group across 1Y / 3Y / 5Y / 10Y. Use percentile and quartile rank: top quartile is above-average, second quartile near-average-but-better, third quartile below-average-but-acceptable, bottom quartile materially weak. Always report peer-group size alongside the rank — median in a 600-fund category is different from median in a 10-fund category. Track whether standing is improving, stable, or deteriorating and cite the actual percentile movement. For a passive index fund inside an active-heavy peer category, the structural tracking-cost headwind active managers carry means median-among-active is a Pass-grade outcome — do not Fail a passive fund on peer rank alone. Pass when the fund is in the top two quartiles over the longest available window AND the percentile trend is not deteriorating sharply. Fail when it sits in the bottom quartile across multiple windows without a mandate-based reason (e.g. a long-vol fund in the bottom quartile of a multi-strategy category during an equity bull is mandate-aligned, not a Fail).
factors.factorMetrics: percentileRanks, quartileRanks, numberOfInvestmentsInCategory, overviewCategory, returnVsCategory, riskVsCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Use the ETF's exact Morningstar category from `overviewCategory` — broad-equity splits by size, style, and region. For passive index funds in an active-heavy peer category, median is a Pass-grade outcome because active managers carry a structural fee + tracking-cost headwind. Quote the rank across multiple windows (e.g. `1Y: 32, 3Y: 18, 5Y: 14`) — a deteriorating sequence is a yellow flag even if the latest window looks fine.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Use the ETF's exact category from `overviewCategory`. Sector categories (large defined sectors) tend to be tight peer groups; thematic and miscellaneous-sector categories are more dispersed; single-country EM categories have small peer counts — always report the peer count alongside the rank. Quote the rank across multiple windows (e.g. `1Y: 32, 3Y: 18, 5Y: 14`) — a deteriorating sequence is a yellow flag even if the latest window looks fine.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Leveraged and inverse peer categories are small. Rank between products inside the same leverage bucket is mostly about daily-tracking quality and issuer execution — structural decay applies to every product in the category, so do not Fail a fund on rank alone if the decay is in line with peers.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Categories here are small (broad commodities, precious metals, digital assets, single-currency) — report peer count alongside any rank. Physical-backed vs futures-based wrappers within the same category have different cost / tracking profiles — note which sub-group of peers the rank is actually comparing against.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Use the ETF's exact category from `overviewCategory` — derivative-income and alternative strategies split into several distinct peer groups by strategy type. Inside derivative-income especially, peer dispersion is wide because different funds use different option mechanics and underlying indices.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Each target-date vintage is its own peer group, and static allocation funds split by equity-bond mix (conservative / moderate / aggressive) and by tactical or global variants. Stay inside the specific category named in `overviewCategory`.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Bond categories are duration-defined and split between government, core, inflation-protected, and muni (national vs single-state). Compare within the exact category named in the ETF's `overviewCategory`.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Credit-and-income categories split by credit tier and asset type (high-yield corporate, bank loan, preferred, convertible, EM debt, multisector, nontraditional, securitized, high-yield muni). Compare within the exact category named in the ETF's `overviewCategory`.
